title: api.get_dsep_equations
text:
api.get_dsep_equations
api.get_dsep_equations(equations, latent=None)
Helper to return implied d-sep claims without fitting the model.

title: api.fit
text:
api.fit
api.fit(equations, data, family=None, latent=None, cor_matrices=None, dsep=False, dsep_only=False, calculate_waic=False, num_samples=1000, num_warmup=500, num_chains=1, thinning=1, n_cores=1, seed=0, dsep_max_obs=2000, quiet=False, dsep_equations_to_run=None, adapt_delta=0.95, fix_latent='loading')
High-level API for because-py. Fits a causal hierarchical model using NumPyro.
:param equations: List of formula strings (e.g., [“y ~ x + z + (1|group)”]). :param data: Dictionary of numpy arrays for the data. :param family: Dictionary specifying families (e.g. {“y”: “poisson”}). Default is “gaussian”. :param latent: List of unmeasured/latent variables (e.g. [“L1”]). If None, auto-detected from equations. :param dsep: Boolean, if True, computes and tests the basis set of implied conditional independencies. :param num_samples: Number of post-warmup MCMC samples. :param num_warmup: Number of warmup MCMC samples. :param num_chains: Number of parallel MCMC chains. :param thinning: Thinning interval for MCMC samples. :param seed: Random seed for JAX PRNGKey. :param dsep_max_obs: Maximum number of observations to use for d-sep tests (random subsampling) for speed. :param quiet: Boolean, if True, suppresses MCMC progress bars.
:return: A dictionary containing the fitted MCMC object and optional dsep results.

title: because-py
text:
because-py is the Python inference engine behind the R because package. It translates structural equation models (SEMs) specified as R-style formulas into fully probabilistic NumPyro models and samples them with HMC/NUTS.
You can use because-py in two ways:

title: because-py
text:
Installation
See the Installation & Setup article for full instructions including JAX/GPU configuration.
pip install because_py

title: because-py
text:
How it works
because-py parses SEM formulas into a directed acyclic graph (DAG), topologically sorts the nodes, and generates a NumPyro model closure on the fly. The model is then sampled with NUTS (No-U-Turn Sampler) via JAX — supporting CPU, GPU, and TPU backends.
For a full description of the statistical framework, see the because package documentation.

title: Installation & Setup
text: because-py is the Python NumPyro backend for the R because package. It can be used from R (the recommended approach) or directly in Python.

title: Installation & Setup
text:
because-py requires Python ≥ 3.8 and the following dependencies, which are installed automatically:

JAX + jaxlib
NumPyro
NumPy

title: Installation & Setup
text: because-py is the Python NumPyro backend for the R because package. It can be used from R (the recommended approach) or directly in Python.

title: Installation & Setup
text:
Using because-py from R
because-py is designed to be called from R via the because package using reticulate.

Step 1 — Install the R packages
install.packages("because")       # from CRAN
install.packages("reticulate")    # if not already installed


Step 2 — Set up a Python environment
library(reticulate)

# Create a dedicated conda environment (recommended)
conda_create("because-env", python_version = "3.11")
use_condaenv("because-env")

# Or use a virtualenv
virtualenv_create("because-env")
use_virtualenv("because-env")


Step 3 — Install because-py and JAX into that environment
py_install("because_py", pip = TRUE)
py_install(c("jax", "jaxlib"), pip = TRUE)


Step 4 — Verify
library(because)
reticulate::py_config()   # should show your because-env Python
Then run any because() call with engine = "numpyro":
fit <- because(
  equations = list(y ~ x),
  data      = mydata,
  engine    = "numpyro",
  n.iter    = 2000
)

title: Installation & Setup
text:
JAX configuration

CPU (default)
No extra configuration needed.


GPU (NVIDIA)
pip install --upgrade "jax[cuda12_pip]" \
  -f https://storage.googleapis.com/jax-releases/jax_cuda_releases.html


Apple Silicon (M-series)
pip install jax-metal

title: Installation & Setup
text:
Troubleshooting
ImportError: cannot import name 'because' Make sure reticulate is pointing to the Python environment where because_py is installed:
reticulate::use_condaenv("because-env", required = TRUE)
JAX initialization errors on macOS Try setting the platform explicitly:
import jax
jax.config.update("jax_platform_name", "cpu")
or from R before loading the package:
Sys.setenv(JAX_PLATFORMS = "cpu")

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text: The because R package calls because-py transparently whenever you specify engine = "numpyro". You do not need to write any Python code — reticulate handles the bridge. The workflow is identical to a JAGS run: same formula syntax, same summary() and plot_dag() methods.

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text: The because R package calls because-py transparently whenever you specify engine = "numpyro". You do not need to write any Python code — reticulate handles the bridge. The workflow is identical to a JAGS run: same formula syntax, same summary() and plot_dag() methods.

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
A simple causal model

library(because)
library(because.phybase)

# Bird life-history data (678 species)
data(birds)

fit <- because(
  equations = list(
    Brain    ~ Mass + Migration,
    Lifespan ~ Brain,
    Clutch   ~ Brain + Mass + Migration
  ),
  data   = birds,
  engine = "numpyro",
  n.iter = 2000,
  n.burnin = 500
)

summary(fit)

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
D-separation testing first
Following best practice, always test d-separation before comparing models by WAIC. Only models that pass all d-sep tests are structurally valid.

# Run d-sep tests without fitting the full model (fast)
fit_dsep <- because(
  equations = list(
    Brain    ~ Mass + Migration,
    Lifespan ~ Brain,
    Clutch   ~ Brain + Mass + Migration
  ),
  data   = birds,
  engine = "numpyro",
  dsep   = TRUE,
  dsep_only = TRUE
)

fit_dsep$dsep

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
section: Inspecting the generated NumPyro model code
text:
Inspecting the generated NumPyro model code
A unique feature of because-py is that the generated NumPyro model is stored as an executable Python string, which you can inspect and modify:

cat(fit$numpyro_code)

This is useful for:

Verification — confirming the model matches your theoretical expectations
Customisation — copying the code to a .py script and adding custom priors or likelihoods
Teaching — showing students the connection between DAG specification and probabilistic code

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
Comparing models
Once d-sep is confirmed, use WAIC to compare models with the same response variable:

fit_null <- because(
  equations = list(Brain ~ Mass),
  data      = birds,
  engine    = "numpyro",
  n.iter    = 2000
)

because_compare(fit, fit_null)







Important



WAIC comparisons are only valid between models that share the same response variable(s) and the same observations. Cross-model comparisons with different endogenous variables will produce inflated WAIC values.

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
Extending chains with because_continue()
NumPyro’s HMC/NUTS converges so efficiently that because_continue() is rarely needed. If you do need more samples, simply re-run with a larger n.iter:

fit2 <- because(
  equations = list(
    Brain    ~ Mass + Migration,
    Lifespan ~ Brain,
    Clutch   ~ Brain + Mass + Migration
  ),
  data     = birds,
  engine   = "numpyro",
  n.iter   = 5000,   # increase from 2000
  n.burnin = 500
)

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
Visualising the DAG
All plot methods work identically regardless of engine:

plot_dag(fit)

title: Using because-py via R (reticulate)
text:
Traceplots and diagnostics

# Using coda
plot(fit$samples)

# Or with bayesplot
library(bayesplot)
mcmc_trace(fit$samples, pars = c("beta_Brain_Mass", "beta_Brain_Migration"))
mcmc_areas(fit$samples, pars = c("beta_Brain_Mass", "beta_Brain_Migration"))

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text: because-py exposes a fit() function that accepts formula strings and NumPy arrays. This is useful when you want to integrate causal modelling into a Python pipeline (e.g., alongside pandas, scikit-learn, or ArviZ).

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text: because-py exposes a fit() function that accepts formula strings and NumPy arrays. This is useful when you want to integrate causal modelling into a Python pipeline (e.g., alongside pandas, scikit-learn, or ArviZ).

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
Basic usage
import numpy as np
import because

# Simulate some data
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
n   = 200
x   = rng.normal(size=n)
y   = 0.7 * x + rng.normal(size=n)

result = because.fit(
    equations   = ["y ~ x"],
    data        = {"x": x, "y": y},
    num_samples = 1000,
    num_warmup  = 500,
    num_chains  = 4
)

# Posterior summary
import arviz as az
idata = az.from_numpyro(result["mcmc"])
print(az.summary(idata, var_names=["beta_y_x", "alpha_y"]))

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
Specifying families
Pass a family dictionary to use non-Gaussian likelihoods:
result = because.fit(
    equations = ["y ~ x + z"],
    data      = {"x": x, "z": z, "y": y_counts},
    family    = {"y": "poisson"},
    num_samples = 2000
)
Supported families: "gaussian", "poisson", "binomial", "negbinomial", "ordinal".

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
D-separation testing
dsep_result = because.fit(
    equations = ["Brain ~ Mass + Migration",
                 "Lifespan ~ Brain",
                 "Clutch  ~ Brain + Mass + Migration"],
    data      = data_dict,
    dsep      = True,
    dsep_only = True   # skip MCMC, only run d-sep tests
)

for test in dsep_result["dsep"]:
    print(test)

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
Accessing the generated model code
result = because.fit(
    equations   = ["y ~ x"],
    data        = {"x": x, "y": y},
    num_samples = 1000
)

# Print the auto-generated NumPyro model
print(result["model_code"])

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
Using ArviZ for diagnostics
import arviz as az

idata = az.from_numpyro(result["mcmc"])

# Trace plots
az.plot_trace(idata)

# R-hat and ESS
print(az.summary(idata))

# WAIC
print(az.waic(idata))

title: Using because-py directly in Python
text:
GPU / multi-device sampling
JAX handles device placement automatically. To run 4 chains on 4 GPUs:
import jax
print(jax.devices())   # check available devices

result = because.fit(
    equations  = ["y ~ x"],
    data       = data_dict,
    num_chains = 4,       # one chain per device when possible
    n_cores    = 4
)

title: builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder
text:
builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder(self, causal_graph, family_dict=None, deterministic_terms=None, cor_matrices=None, fix_latent='loading')
Takes a parsed causal graph and generates the dynamic NumPyro model via a closure.





Name
Description




generate_model_code_string
Returns a self-contained, executable Python source string that


generate_model_function
Returns a callable python function representing the NumPyro model.





builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder.generate_model_code_string(data_shapes=None)
Returns a self-contained, executable Python source string that represents the NumPyro model built by generate_model_function().
The output is intended for inspection, auditing, or copy-paste modification in a standalone Python script. Covariance / Cholesky matrices are referenced as arguments so the function signature matches what because-py passes internally.
:param data_shapes: Optional dict mapping variable names to their shapes (e.g. {“Brain”: (678,), “phy”: (678, 678)}). Used to annotate the function signature with dimension comments. :return: str — a Python source string.



builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder.generate_model_function(data_for_compilation=None, force_plate_obs=False)
Returns a callable python function representing the NumPyro model. :param data_for_compilation: Optional dict of the data to statically optimize the JAX trace (e.g. for NaN masking). :param force_plate_obs: If True, forces the use of numpyro.plate for observations even if multiPhylo is detected. Used for WAIC calculation.

The output is intended for inspection, auditing, or copy-paste modification in a standalone Python script. Covariance / Cholesky matrices are referenced as arguments so the function signature matches what because-py passes internally.
:param data_shapes: Optional dict mapping variable names to their shapes (e.g. {“Brain”: (678,), “phy”: (678, 678)}). Used to annotate the function signature with dimension comments. :return: str — a Python source string.

Returns a callable python function representing the NumPyro model. :param data_for_compilation: Optional dict of the data to statically optimize the JAX trace (e.g. for NaN masking). :param force_plate_obs: If True, forces the use of numpyro.plate for observations even if multiPhylo is detected. Used for WAIC calculation.

title: API Reference
text:
Main entry point for fitting causal models with NumPyro.



api.fit
High-level API for because-py. Fits a causal hierarchical model using NumPyro.


api.get_dsep_equations
Helper to return implied d-sep claims without fitting the model.






Lower-level classes for building and inspecting NumPyro models.



builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder
Takes a parsed causal graph and generates the dynamic NumPyro model via a closure.

text:
Main entry point for fitting causal models with NumPyro.



api.fit
High-level API for because-py. Fits a causal hierarchical model using NumPyro.


api.get_dsep_equations
Helper to return implied d-sep claims without fitting the model.

title: API Reference
text:
Lower-level classes for building and inspecting NumPyro models.



builder.compiler.NumPyroBuilder
Takes a parsed causal graph and generates the dynamic NumPyro model via a closure.

title: Installation & Setup
text:
From R (recommended)

Step 1 — Install the because R package
# Stable release
remotes::install_github("because-pkg/because@v1.3.0", build_vignettes = TRUE)

# Latest development version
remotes::install_github("because-pkg/because", build_vignettes = TRUE)


Step 2 — Install reticulate
install.packages("reticulate")


Step 3 — Install Python (if needed)
If you do not already have Python on your system, reticulate can install Miniconda for you (skip this if Python is already installed):
reticulate::install_miniconda()


Step 4 — One-step setup
because provides a built-in helper that installs all required Python packages (numpyro, jax, jaxlib, networkx, funsor, and because_py) into your active Python environment:
install_because_numpyro()
The function automatically detects your active Python environment — including RStudio project-level virtual environments (.venv) and any existing reticulate configuration — falling back to "r-reticulate" if none is found.


Troubleshooting: wrong Python environment
If in a fresh R session reticulate picks up a different Python environment than the one where the packages were installed, because will throw:
Error: Failed to import python module 'because.api'.
Python is currently running from: /path/to/wrong/python
Ensure because_py is installed in this exact environment.
Try running: install_because_numpyro()
In this case, point reticulate to the correct environment before calling because():
reticulate::use_virtualenv("r-reticulate", required = TRUE)

title: Installation & Setup
text:
Directly in Python
pip install because_py
Or from source:
git clone https://github.com/because-pkg/because_py.git
cd because_py
pip install -e .
